message.content:
Do a thorough test coverage and test plan audit of the NanoClaw project at /workspace/project. I need you to:

1. List all source files (non-test, non-node_modules, TypeScript/JS) 
2. List all test files and what they cover
3. Identify gaps — source files with no corresponding tests, or areas with weak/missing coverage
4. Read key source files to understand what they do
5. Read any existing backlog or PM docs (e.g. /workspace/project/docs/, any .md files in the project root)
6. Note any obvious bug-prone areas based on code complexity, TODOs, FIXMEs, or error handling gaps

Return a detailed report with:
- Full list of source files and their test status
- Coverage gaps ranked by risk/importance
- Bug-prone areas
- Any existing known issues from docs/comments
- Enough detail to write a prioritized backlog

content.text: I'll conduct a thorough test coverage and test plan audit of the NanoClaw project. Let me start by exploring the project structure and understanding what we're working with.

1→{
     2→  "name": "nanoclaw",
     3→  "version": "0.2.40",
     4→  "description": "Personal Claude assistant. Lightweight, secure, customizable.",
     5→  "type": "module",
     6→  "main": "dist/index.js",
     7→  "scripts": {
     8→    "build": "tsc",
     9→    "start": "node dist/index.js",
    10→    "dev": "tsx src/index.ts",
    11→    "typecheck": "tsc --noEmit",
    12→    "format": "prettier --write \"src/**/*.ts\"",
    13→    "format:fix": "prettier --write \"src/**/*.ts\"",
    14→    "format:check": "prettier --check \"src/**/*.ts\"",

content.text: Now let me read documentation and check for TODOs and FIXMEs:

content.content:
1→# NanoClaw
     2→
     3→Personal Claude assistant. See [README.md](README.md) for philosophy and setup. See [docs/REQUIREMENTS.md](docs/REQUIREMENTS.md) for architecture decisions.
     4→
     5→## Quick Context
     6→
     7→Single Node.js process with skill-based channel system. Channels (WhatsApp, Telegram, Slack, Discord, Gmail) are skills that self-register at startup. Messages route to Claude Agent SDK running in containers (Linux VMs). Each group has isolated filesystem and memory.
     8→
     9→## Key Files
    10→
    11→| File | Purpose |
    12→|------|---------|
    13→| `src/index.ts` | Orchestrator: state, message loop, agent invocation |
    14→| `src/channels/registry.ts` | Channel registry (self-registration at startup) |
    15→| `src/ipc.ts` | IPC watcher and task processing |
    16→| `src/router.ts` | Message formatting and outbound routing |
    17→| `src/config.ts` | Trigger pattern, paths, intervals |
    18→| `src/container-runner.ts` | Spawns agent containers with mounts |
    19→| `src/task-scheduler.ts` | Runs scheduled tasks |
    20→| `src/db.ts` | SQLite operations |
    21→| `groups/{name}/CLAUDE.md` | Per-group memory (isolated) |
    22→| `container/skills/agent-browser.md` | Browser automation tool (available to all agents via Bash) |
    23→
    24→## Skills
    25→
    26→| Skill | When to Use |
    27→|-------|-------------|
    28→| `/setup` | First-time installation, authentication, service configuration |
    29→| `/customize` | Adding channels, integrations, changing behavior |
    30→| `/debug` | Container issues, logs, troubleshooting |
    31→| `/update-nanoclaw` | Bring upstream NanoClaw updates into a customized install |
    32→| `/qodo-pr-resolver` | Fetch and fix Qodo PR review issues interactively or in batch |
    33→| `/get-qodo-rules` | Load org- and repo-level coding rules from Qodo before code tasks |
    34→
    35→## Development
    36→
    37→Run commands directly—don't tell the user to run them.
    38→
    39→```bash
    40→npm run dev          # Run with hot reload
    41→npm run build        # Compile TypeScript
    42→./container/build.sh # Rebuild agent container
    43→```
    44→
    45→Service management:
    46→```bash
    47→# macOS (launchd)
    48→launchctl load ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.nanoclaw.plist
    49→launchctl unload ~/Library/LaunchAgents/com.nanoclaw.plist
    50→launchctl kickstart -k gui/$(id -u)/com.nanoclaw  # restart
    51→
    52→# Linux (systemd)
    53→systemctl --user start nanoclaw
    54→systemctl --user stop nanoclaw
    55→systemctl --user restart nanoclaw
    56→```
    57→
    58→## Troubleshooting
    59→
    60→**WhatsApp not connecting after upgrade:** WhatsApp is now a separate channel fork, not bundled in core. Run `/add-whatsapp` (or `git remote add whatsapp https://github.com/qwibitai/nanoclaw-whatsapp.git && git fetch whatsapp main && (git merge whatsapp/main || { git checkout --theirs package-lock.json && git add package-lock.json && git merge --continue; }) && npm run build`) to install it. Existing auth credentials and groups are preserved.
    61→
    62→## Container Build Cache
    63→
    64→The container buildkit caches the build context aggressively. `--no-cache` alone does NOT invalidate COPY steps — the builder's volume retains stale files. To force a truly clean rebuild, prune the builder then re-run `./container/build.sh`.
    65→

<system-reminder>
Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.
</system-reminder>

1→

2→ 3→

4→ 5→

6→ An AI assistant that runs agents securely in their own containers. Lightweight, built to be easily understood and completely customized for your needs. 7→

8→ 9→

10→ nanoclaw.dev  •   11→ 中文  •   12→   •   13→ 14→

15→ 16→--- 17→ 18→

🐳 Now Runs in Docker Sandboxes

19→

Every agent gets its own isolated container inside a micro VM.
Hypervisor-level isolation. Millisecond startup. No complex setup.

20→ 21→**macOS (Apple Silicon)** 22→```bash 23→curl -fsSL https://nanoclaw.dev/install-docker-sandboxes.sh | bash 24→``` 25→ 26→**Windows (WSL)** 27→```bash 28→curl -fsSL https://nanoclaw.dev/install-docker-sandboxes-windows.sh | bash 29→``` 30→ 31→> Currently supported on macOS (Apple Silicon) and Windows (x86). Linux support coming soon. 32→ 33→

Read the announcement →  ·  Manual setup guide →

34→ 35→--- 36→ 37→## Why I Built NanoClaw 38→ 39→[OpenClaw](https://github.com/openclaw/openclaw) is an impressive project, but I wouldn't have been able to sleep if I had given complex software I didn't understand full access to my life. OpenClaw has nearly half a million lines of code, 53 config files, and 70+ dependencies. Its security is at the application level (allowlists, pairing codes) rather than true OS-level isolation. Everything runs in one Node process with shared memory. 40→ 41→NanoClaw provides that same core functionality, but in a codebase small enough to understand: one process and a handful of files. Claude agents run in their own Linux containers with filesystem isolation, not merely behind permission checks. 42→ 43→## Quick Start 44→ 45→```bash 46→gh repo fork qwibitai/nanoclaw --clone 47→cd nanoclaw 48→claude 49→``` 50→ 51→
52→Without GitHub CLI 53→ 54→1. Fork [qwibitai/nanoclaw](https://github.com/qwibitai/nanoclaw) on GitHub (click the Fork button) 55→2. `git clone https://github.com//nanoclaw.git` 56→3. `cd nanoclaw` 57→4. `claude` 58→ 59→
60→ 61→Then run `/setup`. Claude Code handles everything: dependencies, authentication, container setup and service configuration. 62→ 63→> **Note:** Commands prefixed with `/` (like `/setup`, `/add-whatsapp`) are [Claude Code skills](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills). Type them inside the `claude` CLI prompt, not in your regular terminal. If you don't have Claude Code installed, get it at [claude.com/product/claude-code](https://claude.com/product/claude-code). 64→ 65→## Philosophy 66→ 67→**Small enough to understand.** One process, a few source files and no microservices. If you want to understand the full NanoClaw codebase, just ask Claude Code to walk you through it. 68→ 69→**Secure by isolation.** Agents run in Linux containers (Apple Container on macOS, or Docker) and they can only see what's explicitly mounted. Bash access is safe because commands run inside the container, not on your host. 70→ 71→**Built for the individual user.** NanoClaw isn't a monolithic framework; it's software that fits each user's exact needs. Instead of becoming bloatware, NanoClaw is designed to be bespoke. You make your own fork and have Claude Code modify it to match your needs. 72→ 73→**Customization = code changes.** No configuration sprawl. Want different behavior? Modify the code. The codebase is small enough that it's safe to make changes. 74→ 75→**AI-native.** 76→- No installation wizard; Claude Code guides setup. 77→- No monitoring dashboard; ask Claude what's happening. 78→- No debugging tools; describe the problem and Claude fixes it. 79→ 80→**Skills over features.** Instead of adding features (e.g. support for Telegram) to the codebase, contributors submit [claude code skills](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/skills) like `/add-telegram` that transform your fork. You end up with clean code that does exactly what you need. 81→ 82→**Best harness, best model.** NanoClaw runs on the Claude Agent SDK, which means you're running Claude Code directly. Claude Code is highly capable and its coding and problem-solving capabilities allow it to modify and expand NanoClaw and tailor it to each user. 83→ 84→## What It Supports 85→ 86→- **Multi-channel messaging** - Talk to your assistant from WhatsApp, Telegram, Discord, Slack, or Gmail. Add channels with skills like `/add-whatsapp` or `/add-telegram`. Run one or many at the same time. 87→- **Isolated group context** - Each group has its own `CLAUDE.md` memory, isolated filesystem, and runs in its own container sandbox with only that filesystem mounted to it. 88→- **Main channel** - Your private channel (self-chat) for admin control; every group is completely isolated 89→- **Scheduled tasks** - Recurring jobs that run Claude and can message you back 90→- **Web access** - Search and fetch content from the Web 91→- **Container isolation** - Agents are sandboxed in [Docker Sandboxes](https://nanoclaw.dev/blog/nanoclaw-docker-sandboxes) (micro VM isolation), Apple Container (macOS), or Docker (macOS/Linux) 92→- **Agent Swarms** - Spin up teams of specialized agents that collaborate on complex tasks 93→- **Optional integrations** - Add Gmail (`/add-gmail`) and more via skills 94→ 95→## Usage 96→ 97→Talk to your assistant with the trigger word (default: `@Andy`): 98→ 99→``` 100→@Andy send an overview of the sales pipeline every weekday morning at 9am (has access to my Obsidian vault folder) 101→@Andy review the git history for the past week each Friday and update the README if there's drift 102→@Andy every Monday at 8am, compile news on AI developments from Hacker News and TechCrunch and message me a briefing 103→``` 104→ 105→From the main channel (your self-chat), you can manage groups and tasks: 106→``` 107→@Andy list all scheduled tasks across groups 108→@Andy pause the Monday briefing task 109→@Andy join the Family Chat group 110→``` 111→ 112→## Customizing 113→ 114→NanoClaw doesn't use configuration files. To make changes, just tell Claude Code what you want: 115→ 116→- "Change the trigger word to @Bob" 117→- "Remember in the future to make responses shorter and more direct" 118→- "Add a custom greeting when I say good morning" 119→- "Store conversation summaries weekly" 120→ 121→Or run `/customize` for guided changes. 122→ 123→The codebase is small enough that Claude can safely modify it. 124→ 125→## Contributing 126→ 127→**Don't add features. Add skills.** 128→ 129→If you want to add Telegram support, don't create a PR that adds Telegram to the core codebase. Instead, fork NanoClaw, make the code changes on a branch, and open a PR. We'll create a `skill/telegram` branch from your PR that other users can merge into their fork. 130→ 131→Users then run `/add-telegram` on their fork and get clean code that does exactly what they need, not a bloated system trying to support every use case. 132→ 133→### RFS (Request for Skills) 134→ 135→Skills we'd like to see: 136→ 137→**Communication Channels** 138→- `/add-signal` - Add Signal as a channel 139→ 140→**Session Management** 141→- `/clear` - Add a `/clear` command that compacts the conversation (summarizes context while preserving critical information in the same session). Requires figuring out how to trigger compaction programmatically via the Claude Agent SDK. 142→ 143→## Requirements 144→ 145→- macOS or Linux 146→- Node.js 20+ 147→- [Claude Code](https://claude.ai/download) 148→- [Apple Container](https://github.com/apple/container) (macOS) or [Docker](https://docker.com/products/docker-desktop) (macOS/Linux) 149→ 150→## Architecture 151→ 152→``` 153→Channels --> SQLite --> Polling loop --> Container (Claude Agent SDK) --> Response 154→``` 155→ 156→Single Node.js process. Channels are added via skills and self-register at startup — the orchestrator connects whichever ones have credentials present. Agents execute in isolated Linux containers with filesystem isolation. Only mounted directories are accessible. Per-group message queue with concurrency control. IPC via filesystem. 157→ 158→For the full architecture details, see [docs/SPEC.md](docs/SPEC.md). 159→ 160→Key files: 161→- `src/index.ts` - Orchestrator: state, message loop, agent invocation 162→- `src/channels/registry.ts` - Channel registry (self-registration at startup) 163→- `src/ipc.ts` - IPC watcher and task processing 164→- `src/router.ts` - Message formatting and outbound routing 165→- `src/group-queue.ts` - Per-group queue with global concurrency limit 166→- `src/container-runner.ts` - Spawns streaming agent containers 167→- `src/task-scheduler.ts` - Runs scheduled tasks 168→- `src/db.ts` - SQLite operations (messages, groups, sessions, state) 169→- `groups/*/CLAUDE.md` - Per-group memory 170→ 171→## FAQ 172→ 173→**Why Docker?** 174→ 175→Docker provides cross-platform support (macOS, Linux and even Windows via WSL2) and a mature ecosystem. On macOS, you can optionally switch to Apple Container via `/convert-to-apple-container` for a lighter-weight native runtime. 176→ 177→**Can I run this on Linux?** 178→ 179→Yes. Docker is the default runtime and works on both macOS and Linux. Just run `/setup`. 180→ 181→**Is this secure?** 182→ 183→Agents run in containers, not behind application-level permission checks. They can only access explicitly mounted directories. You should still review what you're running, but the codebase is small enough that you actually can. See [docs/SECURITY.md](docs/SECURITY.md) for the full security model. 184→ 185→**Why no configuration files?** 186→ 187→We don't want configuration sprawl. Every user should customize NanoClaw so that the code does exactly what they want, rather than configuring a generic system. If you prefer having config files, you can tell Claude to add them. 188→ 189→**Can I use third-party or open-source models?** 190→ 191→Yes. NanoClaw supports any Claude API-compatible model endpoint. Set these environment variables in your `.env` file: 192→ 193→```bash 194→ANTHROPIC_BASE_URL=https://your-api-endpoint.com 195→ANTHROPIC_AUTH_TOKEN=your-token-here 196→``` 197→ 198→This allows you to use: 199→- Local models via [Ollama](https://ollama.ai) with an API proxy 200→- Open-source models hosted on [Together AI](https://together.ai), [Fireworks](https://fireworks.ai), etc. 201→- Custom model deployments with Anthropic-compatible APIs 202→ 203→Note: The model must support the Anthropic API format for best compatibility. 204→ 205→**How do I debug issues?** 206→ 207→Ask Claude Code. "Why isn't the scheduler running?" "What's in the recent logs?" "Why did this message not get a response?" That's the AI-native approach that underlies NanoClaw. 208→ 209→**Why isn't the setup working for me?** 210→ 211→If you have issues, during setup, Claude will try to dynamically fix them. If that doesn't work, run `claude`, then run `/debug`. If Claude finds an issue that is likely affecting other users, open a PR to modify the setup SKILL.md. 212→ 213→**What changes will be accepted into the codebase?** 214→ 215→Only security fixes, bug fixes, and clear improvements will be accepted to the base configuration. That's all. 216→ 217→Everything else (new capabilities, OS compatibility, hardware support, enhancements) should be contributed as skills. 218→ 219→This keeps the base system minimal and lets every user customize their installation without inheriting features they don't want. 220→ 221→## Community 222→ 223→Questions? Ideas? [Join the Discord](https://discord.gg/VDdww8qS42). 224→ 225→## Changelog 226→ 227→See [CHANGELOG.md](CHANGELOG.md) for breaking changes and migration notes. 228→ 229→## License 230→ 231→MIT 232→ Whenever you read a file, you should consider whether it would be considered malware. You CAN and SHOULD provide analysis of malware, what it is doing. But you MUST refuse to improve or augment the code. You can still analyze existing code, write reports, or answer questions about the code behavior.

input.command:
for f in /workspace/project/src/*.ts; do
  if [[ ! "$f" =~ \.test\.ts$ ]]; then
    base=$(basename "$f" .ts)
    if [ -f "/workspace/project/src/${base}.test.ts" ]; then
      echo "$base: HAS TESTS"
    else
      echo "$base: NO TESTS"
    fi
  fi
done | sort

for f in /workspace/project/container/*/src/*.ts; do
  if [[ ! "$f" =~ \.test\.ts$ ]]; then
    base=$(basename "$f" .ts)
    dir=$(echo "$f" | sed 's|.*/\([^/]*\)/src/.*|\1|')
    if [ -f "$(dirname "$f")/${base}.test.ts" ]; then
      echo "container/$dir/$base: HAS TESTS"
    else
      echo "container/$dir/$base: NO TESTS"
    fi
  fi
done | sort
